perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall disclose the Information to their employees, Affiliates, external experts and/or consultants only on a need to know basis and only to the extent absolutely necessary for the Purpose. Each Party shall require that its external experts and/or consultants also have such policies and procedures in place to ensure their compliance with these confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties shall endeavor to disclose the Information to their employees, Affiliates, external experts and/or consultants on what they believe to be a need to know basis and to the extent they deem necessary for the Purpose. Each Party should encourage its external experts and/or consultants to adopt policies and procedures to generally comply with these confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall disclose' to 'shall endeavor to disclose,' 'need to know basis' to 'what they believe to be a need to know basis,' 'absolutely necessary' to 'they deem necessary,' 'shall require' to 'should encourage,' and 'ensure their compliance' to 'generally comply' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets and requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. By weakening the obligation to disclose only on a strict 'need to know' basis and diluting the compliance requirements, the modified text contradicts the reasonable efforts standard required to maintain trade secret protection under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially contradicting the reasonable efforts standard required to maintain trade secret protection under California law. The original text used mandatory terms like 'shall disclose' and 'absolutely necessary,' creating a clear obligation. The changed text uses terms like 'shall endeavor to disclose' and 'they deem necessary,' making the obligation ambiguous. California Civil Code 3426.1 defines 'misappropriation' as the disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent, especially when the knowledge was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy. By weakening the language, the modified text arguably makes it more difficult to prove a breach of the duty to maintain secrecy, which is central to trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of [ … ] , without regard to any principle of conflict or choice of laws that would cause the application of the laws of any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be generally interpreted in accordance with the laws of [ … ] , taking into account principles of conflict or choice of laws as deemed appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'governed by' with 'generally interpreted in accordance with' and adding 'taking into account principles of conflict or choice of laws as deemed appropriate' introduces significant ambiguity regarding which laws apply. This allows for subjective interpretation and potential manipulation of the applicable legal framework.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1856 states that the terms of a completely integrated agreement cannot be contradicted by extrinsic evidence. By making the governing law subject to interpretation and potentially other jurisdictions' laws, the modified text allows for challenges to the agreement's enforceability in California, potentially violating this section.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1856 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1856. (a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (f
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding which jurisdiction's laws govern the agreement, potentially allowing extrinsic evidence or other jurisdictions' laws to influence the interpretation, which could contradict California Code of Civil Procedure § 1856. The original text provides a clear choice of law, while the changed text weakens this by stating the agreement is 'generally interpreted in accordance with' and considers 'principles of conflict or choice of laws as deemed appropriate.' This creates ambiguity and opens the door for legal challenges based on external factors, potentially undermining the agreement's enforceability as a completely integrated agreement under CCP 1856(a), which states that terms 'may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement.'

perturbation.original_text: Any dispute that cannot be s ettled amicably between the Parties shall be exclusively resolved by arbitration. The arbitration rules of [ … ] shall be applicable. The place of any hearing shall be [ … ] and the language of the arbitration shall be [ … ] .
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute that the Parties attempt to settle amicably may be resolved by arbitration. The arbitration rules of [ … ] may be applicable. The place of any hearing may be [ … ] and the language of the arbitration may be [ … ] .
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be exclusively resolved' to 'may be resolved,' 'shall be applicable' to 'may be applicable' weakens the mandatory arbitration clause. This introduces ambiguity as to whether disputes must be arbitrated and allows parties to potentially pursue litigation instead. By changing 'shall' to 'may', arbitration is no longer mandatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FAA favors and enforces valid arbitration agreements. By making arbitration optional rather than mandatory, the modification weakens the enforceability of the arbitration clause and potentially conflicts with the FAA's intent to uphold arbitration agreements. The FAA requires that agreements to arbitrate be enforced, and this obligation is weakened by the change.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 inserted “or as otherwise provided in chapter 4” before period at end. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2022 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. L. 117–90 , set out as an Effective Date note under section 401 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The original text mandates arbitration, but the changed text makes it optional. 9 U.S. Code § 2 states that agreements to arbitrate 'shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable.' By changing 'shall' to 'may,' the modified contract weakens the enforceability of the arbitration clause, directly contradicting the FAA's intent as it removes the mandatory aspect of arbitration agreements.

perturbation.original_text: All information disclosed by a Party or by Affiliate s of a Party to the other Party or its respective Affiliates orally, electronically, writing or by any other means during the data sharing negotiations shall be considered as confidential unless expressly stated otherwise by the disclosing Party. All such confidential information shall be referred to hereinafter as " ".
perturbation.changed_text: Information disclosed by a Party to the other Party during the data sharing negotiations may be considered confidential, unless otherwise indicated. Such information may be referred to as confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'All information...shall be considered as confidential' with 'Information...may be considered confidential, unless otherwise indicated' introduces ambiguity regarding what constitutes confidential information. Removing 'orally, electronically, writing or by any other means' limits the scope of protected information. Removing the clause referring to Affiliates of a Party further limits the scope.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Trade Secret Law - Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1(d)(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: California Trade Secret law requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By making the confidentiality of disclosed information optional and limiting its scope, the modification undermines the obligation to protect trade secrets, potentially leading to a violation if reasonable efforts are not demonstrably made to maintain confidentiality under the law.

As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the confidentiality obligation, potentially contradicting California Trade Secret law, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The original text states 'All information...shall be considered as confidential,' while the changed text says 'Information...may be considered confidential.' This change introduces ambiguity and reduces the protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' By making confidentiality optional, the changed text undermines the duty to maintain secrecy, potentially enabling 'improper means' of acquiring trade secrets. The change also limits the scope of protected information by removing 'orally, electronically, writing or by any other means' and the reference to Affiliates of a Party, further weakening the protection of trade secrets.
